review_summary: This unit has sufficient grounding for questions on SimCLR and MoCo design choices. The questions focus on transferable distinctions between in-batch negatives, projection heads, and queue-based contrastive learning.

question_bank.question: In SimCLR, how is a positive pair formed?
choices: By taking two different augmentations of the same image.
By pairing two images that share a manually annotated class label.
By pairing each image with the oldest item in a memory queue.
By using one grayscale image and one depth map from unrelated scenes.
question_bank.explanation: SimCLR creates two transformed views from the same source image. Those two views form the positive pair, while other views in the batch act as negatives.

question_bank.question: Why does SimCLR use a projection head before applying the contrastive loss?
choices: So the contrastive loss acts on z while h can preserve richer information for downstream tasks.
So the model can replace all augmentations with manual labels.
So the encoder output h is forced to contain only rotation labels.
So the batch no longer needs any negative samples.
question_bank.explanation: The projection head maps representations into a space used for contrastive training. This can let the upstream representation retain information that may help downstream tasks.

transcript_quotes: preserve all of those extra features

question_bank.question: What problem does MoCo address compared with relying only on SimCLR-style in-batch negatives?
choices: It supplies many negatives through a queue and a momentum-updated key encoder without requiring huge minibatches.
It eliminates contrastive learning by replacing positives with reconstruction targets.
It requires all negatives to be backpropagated through the query encoder.
It uses only one augmented view per image and no stored keys.
question_bank.explanation: MoCo decouples the number of negatives from the current minibatch by storing key representations in a queue. A momentum-updated key encoder keeps those stored negatives compatible over time.
